narrative: SAIL’s FY2025 ESG profile was mixed, with 34 rank-one outcomes but 28 rank-four outcomes. Its standalone reporting boundary remained broad, covering integrated steel plants, special steel plants, units and captive mines, and it obtained reasonable assurance for the BRSR. Environmentally, SAIL showed notable improvement in total energy and physical energy intensity, supported by efficiency and decarbonisation measures identified in the benchmark analysis, including CDQ, TRT, CDI, waste heat recovery, renewable capacity plans and green hydrogen pilots. It also led SOx and NOx emissions in FY2025, a major improvement in local air-emissions positioning. However, it became the absolute water-withdrawal and discharge laggard, remained weak on Scope 2 and physical GHG intensity, and had the weakest total waste disposal due to old-stock landfill. Social performance was split. SAIL led wellbeing expenditure at 1.44% of revenue and female wage share, but reported zero female Board representation and continued to have worker fatalities. Training remained a persistent weakness: 100% worker H&S coverage was not matched by employee H&S, employee skills or worker skill-upgradation reporting. Governance remained comparatively clean on RPT concentration because of its PSU structure, but stakeholder and product-responsibility indicators deteriorated, especially sustainable sourcing, value-chain assessment and product-information coverage. FY2025 positions SAIL as improving operationally in energy and air, but lagging in water, training, inclusion governance and supplier ESG maturity.
